bull.b: A >$170B commercial services backlog provides multi-year visibility into recurring aftermarket revenue, insulating GE from cyclicality in new engine sales. This durable installed-base monetization supports stable cash generation and long-term customer lock-in through MRO and long-term service agreements.

Consistently strong gross and net margins reflect technical leadership in engine design and high-value aftermarket services. Margin durability supports reinvestment, funding of shop capacity and R&D, and underpins resilient returns even as delivery mix shifts between OE and services over the medium term.

Bringing a senior Microsoft AI executive to the board signals structural investment in predictive maintenance, supply orchestration and shop efficiency. Institutionalizing AI in Flight Deck and operations can sustainably reduce repair times, increase shop throughput and lower unit service costs over years.

bear.b: Leverage near parity with equity constrains financial flexibility for large capex, M&A or prolonged downturns. While debt has been cut from 2021 highs, sustained investment cycles and cyclical demand risk leaving the company exposed to refinancing or rating pressure during industry stress.

A material TTM FCF decline despite positive absolute FCF highlights timing and working-capital sensitivity. Inconsistent cash conversion can impede steady deleveraging, limit funding for sustained shop scale-up investments, and increase reliance on external financing during ramp cycles.

Rising delinquencies and supplier pinch points weaken the core services flywheel: delayed parts restrict shop throughput, strain customer relationships, and can push revenue recognition out over quarters. Persistent constraints risk margin erosion and slower backlog-to-revenue conversion over the medium term.
